perturbation.original_text: The Parties will comply with all applicable laws in the use of the Confidential Information and performance of this Agreement, including without limitation the EU, UK and U.S. export control and trading sanctions rules and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will comply with all applicable laws in the use of the Confidential Information and performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific mention of export control and trading sanctions rules could be problematic. While the general statement about compliance remains, the removal of the specific reference weakens the emphasis on adhering to these critical regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 CFR § 732.1 - Steps for Using the EAR
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the emphasis on compliance with U.S. export control regulations (EAR) which are crucial when confidential information relates to technologies or products subject to export restrictions. By removing specific mention, the agreement becomes less clear about the need to adhere to these regulations, potentially leading to unintentional violations. The EAR outlines stringent obligations for companies involved in exporting or re-exporting controlled items, information, or software.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a specific obligation to comply with export control and trading sanctions rules, which are significant legal requirements, weakening the original clause. While a general compliance statement remains, the omission of specific regulations creates a loophole. The Law Explanation highlights that EAR outlines stringent obligations for companies involved in exporting controlled items. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this by mentioning the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Removing the specific mention increases the risk of non-compliance and could be interpreted as a less rigorous commitment to export control regulations, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of England and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts for any and all disputes, claims and indifference arising out of, or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of England.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause regarding exclusive jurisdiction in English courts creates ambiguity. Without a specified jurisdiction, disputes could potentially be litigated in multiple jurisdictions, leading to uncertainty and increased costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause, the agreement becomes subject to the Brussels I Regulation (Recast), which dictates jurisdiction in cross-border disputes within the EU. This regulation outlines specific rules for determining jurisdiction based on the domicile of the defendant or other factors. The removal of the clause means parties could potentially sue in various EU member states, contradicting the original intent of having disputes resolved exclusively in English courts. The law states that a jurisdiction clause is valid, so one must exist.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies exclusive jurisdiction in English courts. The changed text removes this clause, potentially subjecting the agreement to the Brussels I Regulation (Recast), as outlined in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains 'Regulation (EU) No 1215/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 December 2012 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters (recast)', indicates that jurisdiction in cross-border disputes within the EU falls under this regulation. The removal of the exclusive jurisdiction clause allows for disputes to be litigated in multiple jurisdictions, contradicting the original intention and potentially violating the established legal framework for jurisdiction as dictated by the Brussels I Regulation when no jurisdiction clause exists. This is a Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction because removing an essential legal clause affects the enforceability and clarity of the jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior written or oral negotiations, commitments, agreement and understandings between the Parties relating to confidentiality and constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to confidentiality. The Agreement w ill not be deemed or construed to be modified, amended, rescinded, cancelled or waived, in whole or in part, except by a writing signed by the Parties’ authorised representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior written or oral negotiations, commitments, agreement and understandings between the Parties relating to confidentiality and constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the text that the agreement cannot be modified without written consent, we allow for possible verbal agreements to change the terms of the contract. Contracts often have clauses that state the contract can not be changed verbally, but only in writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds, though not explicitly requiring all contracts to be in writing, emphasizes the need for written evidence for certain types of agreements, particularly those involving significant value or long duration. Removing the clause that modifications must be in writing contradicts the underlying principle of ensuring clarity and enforceability through written documentation, potentially leading to disputes based on alleged verbal agreements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: of intent even though neither side is bound by any agreement yet. In cases such as: Turriff Construction Ltd v Regalia Knitting Mills Ltd[1] or more recent Alldridge (Builders) Ltd v Grandactual[2] it was assumed that acceptance of letter of intent does not carry contractual effects. According to Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989[3] contract must be made in writing in order to obtain rights to the site. Nevertheless, acquisitions in auctions, implied trusts (Yaxley v Gotts[4]) and grants of lease up to three years do not require contract to be made in writing. Before signing the contract both parties should sign a deed which according to Alldridge (Builders) Ltd v Grandactual[5] transfers all interests to the land to the new owner. Once contract containing all the main heads is signed by both parties and transfer of interest been witnessed and signed contractual agreement is completed. The freehold title transfers from the vendor to the developer once contracts have been completed, and from that point onwards the developer owns the entire risk (Wilkinson, Reed, & Cadman, 2008). If the land title is registered at the Land Register then the new owner will be liable to the risks after gaining the title to the land. Third party rights Realistic land valuation and future business potential cannot be evaluated if rights of third parties are dismissed. New owners after sale agreement can find themselves trapped with interests of other parties. Those parties not included into contractual agreement are called third parties. Of the many types of interest over land belonging to or in the possession of other persons, the most important so far as building developer is concerned are easements and restrictive covenants (Uff, 2005, p. 488). Nevertheless, impact of licences and leases, proprietary estoppels, adverse possession rights or any other deeds which may bind new owners should be seriously considered before proposition to acquire the site. A purpose of the 1925 Law of property Act was to make land more alienable but at the same time protecting third parties interests in the land. The Land Registration Act 2002 concentrates on the registered title which reflects all attachments to the land. The Land Charges Act 1925 (now Land Charges Act 1972) made equitable third parties interests registrable which means purchaser will be bound to them irrespective of notice (Midland Bank Trust Co. v Green[6]). Easement is an interest in land which gives the right to the owner of one piece of land to restrict the use of land which belongs to another owner. The main purpose of all easements is to benefit on dominant land over the servient land which should prevent persons from acquiring any personal benefits. Easements usually are rights of: way, storage, support or light. As well as being created by express or implied grant or reservation, easements may also be acquired by prescription (Galbraith & Stockdale, 2005). If any neighbouring land has been used for more than 20 years with knowledge of land owner as a benefit for dominant land for example trespass, it becomes subject to legal easement. Hence, a right to a view or control over the land cannot be treated as an easement. If any easements are to be found attached to the land, there are legal ways to invalidate them such as: owner of the land can cancel restriction, he also can abandon the easement which would be treated as implied release and if the servient land is being purchased by owner of dominant land. Licenses to use land with legal permission can be created on land where there is no dominant land therefore easements cannot be registered. It is treated as a legal interest in land and will be bind to new owners in the future. In English law it is common to impose contractual obligation via contractual agreement contained within a deed of sale. Such private control over the building plot could affect future usage of that property. Restrictive covenants are treated as deed restri
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly stated that modifications must be in writing, which provides clarity and prevents disputes based on alleged verbal agreements. The changed text removes this requirement, contradicting the principle of ensuring clarity and enforceability through written documentation as suggested by the Law Explanation. While neither snippet explicitly states that contracts or their modifications *must* be in writing, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, in conjunction with the direct removal of the clause requiring written modification, implies that removing such a clause weakens the contract's enforceability under legal standards. The absence of a written modification clause increases the risk of disputes over alleged verbal modifications, rendering the modified contract potentially less legally sound.
